blocks.text: Do you not know that the saints will judge the world? And if the world is to be judged by you, are you incompetent to try trivial cases?
2 : For the idea that (the believers) , see Dan 7.22 (LXX); Wis 3.8; Rev 3.21; 20.4.
Do you not know that we are to judge angels—to say nothing of ordinary matters?
3 : That the angels themselves are subject to judgment, see Jude 6; 2 Pet 2.4.
If you have ordinary cases, then, do you appoint as judges those who have no standing in the church?
4 : The lawsuits in question seem to have been civil , concerned with money or property.
I say this to your shame. Can it be that there is no one among you wise enough to decide between one believer and another,
but a believera goes to court against a believera—and before unbelievers at that?
In fact, to have lawsuits at all with one another is already a defeat for you. Why not rather be wronged? Why not rather be defrauded?
7 : Paul's advice resembles that of Jesus in Mt 5.39–40; Lk 6.28–30: nonresistance.
But you yourselves wrong and defraud—and believers at that.
Do you not know that wrongdoers will not inherit the kingdom of God? Do not be deceived! Fornicators, idolaters, adulterers, male prostitutes, sodomites,

9 : (Gk “malakoi,” lit., “soft men”) were boys and men sodomized by other males, while (Gk “arsenokoitai,” lit., “men who bed males”; a word first found here and based on LXX Lev 18.22; 20.13) were men who exercised the dominant role, pederasts.
thieves, the greedy, drunkards, revilers, robbers—none of these will inherit the kingdom of God.
And this is what some of you used to be. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and in the Spirit of our God.
6.1–11: Lawsuits between believers. Someone to do what should be unthinkable for a Christian: Christian .
9–11 : Catalogues of vices and virtues are known from Jewish and Greek writers, especially the Stoic philosophers.
“All things are lawful for me,” but not all things are beneficial. “All things are lawful for me,” but I will not be dominated by anything.
12 : Paul quotes a slogan of the Corinthians, a proud boast of freedom, asserting liberation from cultural taboos. Paul counters by insisting upon the mutually as the criterion of moral judgment.
“Food is meant for the stomach and the stomach for food,” and God will destroy both one and the other. The body is meant not for fornication but for the Lord, and the Lord for the body.
13 : Another slogan of the Corinthians, with a background in Cynic philosophy. Paul counters by affirming God's power over the body.
And God raised the Lord and will also raise us by his power.
14 : The resurrection of Jesus signals that the body is not meant for corruption but for ongoing life.
Do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ? Should I therefore take the members of Christ and make them members of a prostitute? Never!
15 : Paul appeals to his characteristic idea of the church as the body of Christ (see 12.12–14,27) in order to exclude the possibility of contact with a dangerous polluting agent, the .
Do you not know that whoever is united to a prostitute becomes one body with her? For it is said, “The two shall be one flesh.”
But anyone united to the Lord becomes one spirit with him.
Shun fornication! Every sin that a person commits is outside the body; but the fornicator sins against the body itself.
18 : Cf. 10.14 (5.9). That himself, against his own person (reflected in his ), is an idea with Stoic parallels.
Or do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, which you have from God, and that you are not your own?
19 : Like the community as a whole (cf. 3.16), each believer is .
For you were bought with a price; therefore glorify God in your body.

6.12–20: Christians going to prostitutes. The abrupt beginning of 6.12, and differences of tone and content with 5.1–6.11, suggest that this paragraph originally belonged not here but possibly between 10.22 and 10.23, with which 6.12–20 has much in common: in both passages, the temptation to immorality is strong and Christians are urged to “flee” (6.18; 10.8,14); in both passages, Paul quotes the Corinthian slogan, (6.12; 10.23).
20 : Paul draws upon the custom of sacred manumission: the slave whose freedom was with money previously deposited in the temple treasury was regarded as the property of the god.
